CURRENT AFFAIRS,

The Clongowes affair got a little bit entangled.
The Lord Chief Baron made a speech which, as
reported,  was interpreted by us, and by many others,
as meaning that he offered a gold medal for mathe-
matics with a view to helping Clongowes students to
obtain Fellowships in Trinity Collge. On the face of
it his remarks, as reported, carry no other interprota-
tion, and besides there was the fact that the Lord Chief
Baron is himself a “ Trinity ’ Catholic, and that in
recent years, whilst the general policy u..mongst I.rxsh
‘Catholics was in conformity with the advice and views
of the Irish Hierarchy, yet several Clongowes men
‘haying gone to * Trinity ’—which of itself might not
necessarily have involved Clongowes in any way—but
Clongowes men having gone there, they were, as can be
seen. by reference to a past number of the C’lonyqwm.a/n,
written of in complimentary terms in that publication.

Yol. X, No. (o) DUBLIN, 17th JUNE, 1905, Price One Penny.
» CONTENTS In view of (1) the reported remarks of the Lord Chief

Baron, (2) of the fact that he is a  Trinity ”’ Catho-
lic himself, and (3) of the fact that the Clongowman
frequently wrote laudatory paragraphs about Clongowes
students who had gone to “Trinity,”” the conclusion
was inevitable, from the evidence, that the Lord Chief
Baron intended his medal to facilitate Clongowes boys
in the competition for Trinity Fellowships, and that the
Rector and staff of Clongowes gratefully accepted the
medal proffered for that purpose. There could have.
been no mistaking the meaning and temor of our note
on the matter in last week’s issue, for never having
been to ““ Trinity *’ we have learned to express ourselves
with directness. We meant what we said, and those
who took the ordinary interpretation - derivable from
what we wrote, took the right one; looking over the
matter in the light of recent developments, we have to
say that under like circumstances we would have dealt
with the matter as we in fact did deal with it.

A new fact, however, has come into the case. The
Lord Chief Baron did not mean what his reported words
conveyed plainly to many people.  Though distin-

* guished alike as a lawyer -and as a Catholic who had

been educated in “ Trinity,”” his powers of clear expres-
sion are not on the high level of his other attainments,
and so whilst speaking at the Clongowes Union, the
reports of his remarks that appeared in the press con-
veyed to the public a meaning almost diametrically
opposed to that which the Lord Chief Baron intended
to convey. This fact of course entirely relieves the
situation, though it may not add to the oratorical repu-
tation of the Lord Chief Baron. Of course, we accept
at once the disclaimer which the Lord Chief Baron com-
municated to Very Rev. Dr. Delany, S.J., and which
the latter communicated to the public through the
medium of the Press. In the course of his letter Dr.
Delany says:—“ I have had, however, a communication
from the Lord Chief Baron in which he expresses his
great surprise at the interpretation which has been
put upon his words. He authorises me to state most
cmphatically that he never suggested nor thought of
suggesting that Clongowes’ boys should study mathe-
catics in order to prepare for Fellowships in Trinity
College ; that, in the observations which he made, what
he intended to convey, what he is convinced he did con-
vey, and what many of those present told him they
then understood him to convey, was simply this: That
as regards the study of mathematics the students of
Catholic schools are at this disadvantage, that they
have not the same prizes to induce them to take up
that study which'are offered to students in Protestant
schools, who look forward to Fellowships in Trinity
College, whilst the competition for the Fellowships en-
sures an abundant supply of qualified mathematical
teachers for the Protestant secondary schools. And
that, recognising this disadvantage, and eager to en-
courage in his own old school the study which he had
himself preferred, he proposed to found a medal to be
awarded to the Clongowes student who most excelled in
pure mathematics. Far from connecting it with any
idea of preparation for Trinity College, that he was
well aware that students at Clongowes are being ex-
prossly prepared to pursue their higher studies in Uni-
versity College for the degrees of the Royal University ;
and that he intended that the students so preparing
should compete for the proposed medal, and that he
had asked the Rector of Clongowes to request Professor
Egan, of Uni,versity College, to conduct the examina-
Wik o o0 o

Of course this. disclaimer puts a new face on the
matter, and though those, including ourselves, who

THE LEADER.

misinterpreted the Lord Chief Baron’s offer and its
acceptance of it by the College authorities cannot be
charged with ,doing anything irrational, all sides may
‘congratulate themselves at the happy ending.

‘We have frequently' commented on the manner in
which the Clongownian patted on the back students of
the College who had entered Trinity in spite of the
prevailing attitude of the Catholic Hierarchy and the
Catholic laity. Now the Clongowes authorities an-
nounce three scholarships, ecach value £40 a year,
tenable for three years in University College, Dublin ;
and Dr. Delany, replying in the Press to a.question
asked of him by a correspondent, states that he and
his colleagues hold most cordially with the pronounce-
ment of the Irish bishops in the matter of Catholics
abstaining from Trinity.” We are very glad to
hear this, we welcome the announcement most cor-
dially. Clongowes might be a great Zrish College if it
would. We will not expect to find in the forthcoming
Clongownian any laudatory notices of past students
who have played the part of back sliders by entering
Trinity College like the Chief Baron and Charles O’Con-
nor, K.C. With Clongowes now in line in this matter
the blackleg nature of the act of any Cathlic entering
“ Trinity ” will be all the more emphatic.

‘We have had occasion more than once to comment
severely upon Clongowes, Blackrock, and Castleknock.
However, we desired not any injury to these prominent
educational institutions, but rather that they should
see the errors of their ways, be converted and live as
beneficent and powerful factors, as they ought to be, in
the Irish intellectual and national economy.  The
efforts of the Irish nation to realise itself would go on
in spite of them, but it would be far better if they went
on with their cordial help and powerful support. If,
for instance, they do not throw themselves on their
athletic sides, into the Irish games’ revival, the Trish
games will go on, but unfortunately the large num-
ber of College boys sent out into the Irish world every
year will be.practically lost to that movement—and that
means a loss to the boys, a serious loss to the move-
ment, and a loss to the country.

Similarly, only more so, with what we call generally
the intellectual Irish Revival movement. If the Col-
leges do not mould their students in the Irish Ireland
direction and place the Irish language and history
where they ought to be in the collegiate economy of
«Ireland, the Colleges will continue to turn out boys
who are out of sympathy with the national trend of
things, and that fact will hurt the boys and the
country alike, and help to perpetuate the abnormal and
muddled state of affairs in which it is apparently not
“ respectable ” to be Irish, and in which the popular
attempt to be Irish is quite naturally, if not inevitably,
accompanied by many treacherous temptations calcu-
lated to lead some unthinking persons to the false con-
clusion that in attempting to be Irish they should
avoid being respectable—respectable without any in-
verted commas. Intellectual movements ough to be led
by intellect; but if a considerable number of the
Colleges keep aloof, we may witness the paradox of an
intellectual movement being guided and governed, to
some extent at least, by the gentle arbitrament of the
blackthorn. We welcome the declared attitude to-
wards “ Trinity *’ of the Garrison, of the distinguished
order some of whose members control the destinies of
Clongowes—an order that Ireland is indebted to for
s0 many brilliant contemposary figures in various fields
of Irish public activity. We welcome the generosity
and public spirit of “the College in establishing the
three scholarships tenable at University College. We

. hope that both are only the beginning of a more for-
ward Irish movement, and we trust that one ‘of the
excellent bands trained by the Christian Brothers, and
not a British military band, will discourse sweet music
at the next gathering of the Clongowes Union.

\The Main Street. of Dunleary and Kingstown, was
thronged on Whit-Monday night with a Bank Holiday
crowd.” If people learneq how to be happy though

JuNE 17, 1905.

sober at the All Ireland Bazaar, many of them at
Kingstown on Monday endeavoured to be happy ‘though
more or less drunk. It was a sad day’s outing for
many poor women who took out their husbands for a
day ‘at the seaside; and a woman with her eyes red
from crying following a staggering husband is not a
particularly enlivening sight on a Bank THoliday or
any other day. Many helpless children were to be
pitied.  However, the Bungs of Kingstown did a
roaring trade. It was instructive if not edifying to
stand for half-an-hour observing the merry ’’-making
crowds. We wonder what would the Japs. have
thought of this snap-shot from the Islands of Saints
and Scholars. IHowever, the reflection that sbru'ck us
particularly was—what an uncommon big order it was
to Irish that lot. Kingstown on Whit-Monday could
not truthfully be called the capital of Irish Ireland.

The Bees Wax Candle Co., Ltd., Liverpool, do a
trade, be it great or small, in Ireland in what they
style “Stamped Altar Wax Candles.” They.do a
comparatively large trade in the counties of Wicklow
and Cork. The candles are sold, amongst other ways,
in Ib. packages. We have a lb. package before us.
The label stamped upon the covering has the imprint
of a Dublin printing firm, and it is satisfactory to find
that a Liverpool candle firm has some of its printing
done in Ireland. Of course it is the label that is
printed by an Irish firm; the “stamped altar wax
candles”’ "are mot made in Ireland.  Some careless
people might possibly jump to the conclusion that as
the label is printed in Ireland that the candles were
Irish also, for the name of the candle makers—perhaps
owing to the well-known modesty of British trading
firms—is not set out on the package. In these days of
Irish Industrial effort it is only fair to the Irish candle
manufacturing industry—which happily isa considerable
one—to place the facts before our readers. Those of
our readers who purchase candles of this class should
be careful to see that the candles are made in Ireland.

Some time in April Mr. James Rogers, of Tullamore,
purchased a cap in the establishment of Messrs. Eden
Bros., Westmoreland Street, Dublin for 4s. 6d. He
asked the assistant to show him none but Irish-made
caps. The cap he selected had no maker’s name upon
it, and Mr. Rogers asked the assistant was he quite sure
that the cap was of Irish make. The assistant emphati-
cally said that it was. Mr. Rogers asked for the name
of the factory that produced the cap. The assistant
said that the cap was made in Letterkenny.  Mr.
Rogers evidently was not satisfied in his mind about the
transaction.  He wrote to Father Sweeney, Adm.,
Letterkenny, and received a reply from that clergyman
that there is no woollen or tweed factory in TLetter-
kenny. Mr. Rogers thereupon wrote, under date 18th
May, to Messrs. Eden Bros. for an explanation.

On the 19th May, Mr. John Haldane, Manager, Eden
Bros., replied as follows:— Replying to yours of 18th
we do not recollect the particular cap you bought
from us, but as we always keep a good assortment of
Irish tweed caps, we can safely assume that you were
supplied with what you asked for. We cannot say any-
thing more definite without seeing the cap in question.
We should then be able to say at once if it is Irish or

not. ‘We shall write further in the course of a few
days.”” .

b, Under date 22nd May, Mr. John Haldane wrote :—

We have just received from our agent a letter in refer-
ence to our last order for ¢ Irish tweed caps’ to this
effect—' Some of these tweeds were bought from a Let-
terkon'ny firm and some at Ardara, both in Co. Done-
gal. They are under the impression that there are no

as cottage industry, and, perhaps, you will understand

that we cannot know the maker until we know the goods

On the 5th June, Mr. Rogers replied that he had re-
ceived from Messrs. Eden Bros., “two rather unsatisfac-
tory letters of the 19th and 29nd ult. respectively. As,
110\\{ever, you want to see the cap before definitely re-
ply.mg to my charge, T intend to call on you with the
article to-morrow, when I hope to be in Dublin. The
extract you give from your agent’s communication is
quite meaningless. I shall be glad to know the name of
this agent, as well as of the firm’s at Letterkenny and
abt Ardara, where he purchased for you; and I would
also like the names of the makers of the Irish caps you
stock, and the da e of your most recent orders for same
prior to 15th April last.”

Mr. Rogers duly called at Messrs. Eden Bros., of
Westmoreland Street on Tuesday, the 6th June. He
showed the cap to the manager, who at once admitted
that it was not of Irish manufacture. His explanation
was that it was a mistake on the part of the assistant.
He offered to supply an Irish-made cap in place of the
foreign one that Mr. Rogers had purchased on the de-
claration that it was Irish. The new caps offered, how-
ever, proved to be marked in a similar way as the non-
Inish cap, and they bore no manufacturer’s name. Mr.
Rogers naturally asked how was he to know that these
caps were Irish. The manager said that he should take
his word for it. Mr. Rogers retorted that in view of
what had occurred he would like some more conclusive
evidence.  Would the manager tell in confidence the
name of the maker or the name of the firm from whom
the caps were purchased. The manager declined to give
the information. He also declined to give Mr. Rogers
the names of the manufacturers of the Irish caps which,
in his letter, he had stated that he stocked.

The clerical managers of National Schools of the
City and County of Limerick have not been slow to
express themselves on Rule 1274 as modified.  The
Right Rev. Monsignor Hallinan presided at a recent
meeting held in St. John’s Schools, Limerick. In the
course of a forcible address, Monsignor Hallinan said—
“The country clamoured for the unconditional with-
drawal of the obnoxious rules, and the country was
right. The rules, as modified, have in them all the
inherent vices of the rules as unmodified; the trivial
alterations are introduced for the purpose of allaying
the popular indignation for the present. The rules as
modified simply spread the time for the perpetration of
the bad work—they are meant to prolong the agony.
The country objected to Rule 127 & because it violates
the natural rights of parents of selecting the school they
thought best for their infant boys. Who conferred on
these fourteen gentlemen any right to dictate to the
parents in this or any other city, or parish to send
their boys under 8 to a female school rather than to
a male school? Is it mot an unwarrantable invasion
of the parental rights? And if so—the change in the
age limit from eight to seven years does not minimise
the gravity of the offence.  If this right be conceded
to these gentlemen now what is to prohibit them in the
course of a few years from extending the age to ten or
twelve years once the rule is in operation. Aye, more,
what is to prevent later on some other dilettante edu-
cationists, if it suits their whims, from insisting that
infant girls would be better taught in male schools,
and making rules accordingly.” The following resolu-
fiions were unanimously passed at the mecting:—(1).
That we cannot accept Rule 127  as recently modified,
and ask for its withdrawal; we also hope that Rules
186, 187, and 194 will be withdrawn. (2). That we
undertake to sustain financially any teachers who may
be effected by our refusal to allow Rule 1274 to be

. enforced.”

The Irish L'imes is gleefully looking forward to a new
Split.  The libeller of Canon M‘Inerney winds up a
leading article in its issue of Tuesday in ‘tlns way i —
“We do not know how far Mr. O’Brien’s influence ex-
tends, but with his eloquence, energy, and ﬁuaucxgl Te-
sources it will be odd if he cannot gather round him at
the next General Election a good many supporters out-
side Cork. If so we may look forward to a stirring
period, recalling the days of Parnellite and anti-Par-
nellite, during which quiet-going members of the com-
munity could get on with their business, while the po.h-
ticians were fighting one another.”” No doubt the Zrish
2'imes got on with its business during the weary years
of the Parnell Split, and it would have no objection to
another flare up. We can quite understand that it may
find it difficult to get on with its business to its own
satisfaction in the present state of the country.

Father William Dollard, P.P., Johnville, N.B.,
Canada, in the course of a letter dated 30th May,
writes :—“In renewing my subscription and a con-
tribution to the Language Fund, allow me to say a
few words. = They can be taken as representative of
Ivish feeling in Canada. As to what is called the ¢ inci-
dent,’ it i3 a symptom of the thorough rottenness which
pervades the governing body of the League. Such a
thing as that would have been instantly denounced
and repudiated by any uncorrupted people. The ques-
tion now is, is there saving graco enough in the body
to expel this corruption, to heal this evil? If there
be not. it is “ mene, T'ekel, upharsin’’® with the League.
A friend said to me lately, while discussing this ¢ ibnci<
dent,—* Those people want a Government of their
own. They are utterly unfit for it. Men of that
stamp in power would make a moral and political sink
of the country in a decade. A legitimate and proper
demand for information to be met with blackguardism
in the street! It sounds supremely silly and wicked.
It would not be tolerated for an hour in any society
amongst us. You've tackled bigger snakes—

such as seoininism in all its protean forms, and if you
haven’t killed them they. are, at least, badly scotched.
Keep at the good work in God’s name. What use will
any of us be if we are not only ¢obnoxious’ to black-
guardism but prepared to kick it into the gutter.””
Father Dollard of Canada, encloses 10s. for the Lan-

guage Fund, and sends it through us by way of making
‘his protest.

Mr. William P. Linchan, wholesale newsagent,
309-11 Little Collins St., Melbourne, has sent us a
~ selection of penny publications issued by the Australian
Catholic Truth Society. The series includes “ Rise and
Progress of the Church in Australia,” “The Catholic
Church and Temperance,”’ The Xmas Crib,”  The
Drunkard,’” and ** Mary Immaculate and Democracy.’’
The last-named, by the Most Rev. Dr. Keane, Arch-
bishop, deals in an able manner with the far-reaching
effects of the reverence for the Mother of God on the
progress of civilization.  “The Drunkard’’ is a re-
print of a powerful Temperance sermon preached in
the early part of last century by Archbishop Ulla-
thorne. In an introductory note to this sermon the
curious circumstance is noted that previous to its pub-
lication the substance of it was preached amongst other
places in three different public-houses—once at Appin,
in Cumberland, N.S.W., once at Bathurst, among the
Blue Mountain range, and also at Patrick’s Plains, on
the banks of the River Hunter. A fact which was due
to these localities being at that period without any
more appropriate place in which a congregation could
assemble.

Those of our readers who were present at the Mun-
ster Feis, 1904, will be pleased to learn that this
year’s function will be also held in the Earl of Ken-
mare’s Demesne, but in a different part of these beau-
tiful grounds. The place chosen is within one minute’s
walk of the Railway Station, from which it can be
reached without having to travel through the town of
Killarney. It may be well also to state here for the
information of schools, that in the choral competitions
the Committee has decided that choirs having only one-
third or less of the number which formed a winning
choir at a previous Oireachtas or Munster Feis, will
be regarded as a New Choir, and that if a previously
winning choir competes it will be handicaped by 15
per- cent. of the total marks obtained.

The Syllabus of the Feipr onclaipse (Waterford'

Feis) which will be held in the Courthouse grounds,

- Waterford on Sunday, September 10th, has been pub-
lished. There are 26 competitions in language,
singing, dancing, music, etc., some of which are open to
all Treland. Last year’s Feis, we understand, produced
a large number of entries, and we hope the forthcoming
one will be equally successful.

In the course of a leading article on the Norwegian
affair, the Irish 7'mes complacently remarks.—* At
any time the affairs of Norway and Sweden would in-
terest us. We claim descent from those Norse seamen
who stamped the characteristics of their race upon our
British ancestors, and when we look back over our mari-
time and naval records we cannot fail to be struck by
the reproduction of the Viking traits in our Raleighs,
our Frobishers, our Howards, and our Drakes.””  The L
italics are ours. 1

MR. JOHN DILLON ON CATHOLIC INTERESTS.

R. JOHN DILLON made a curious speech af
l_ Belfast recently. He had something to say
about Tolerance—and Tolerance is a blessed thing that
we have consistently—if we may say so—vigorously
fought for. Mr. Dillon said :—“ Well, I can say this,
at all events, according to the best of my judgment from
my earliest years to this day, I have endeavoured to
champion Catholic interests.””  Of course, we accept Mr.
Dillon’s word ; and we can say this, at all events, that
we have not an unqualified admiration for Mr. Dillon’s
judgment.

Mr. Dillon held—we presume he still holds—such a
large amount of shares in the Great Northern Railway
of Ireland as would render him eligible for a seat on the
Board of Directors. Now the bigotry, the boycott of
Catholics, in the service of that railway is open and pal-
pable to anyone. The awful state of affairs was pub-
lished by us and republished in our reprint— Three
Railways and a Bank.”” Now shareholders have their
duties as well as their dividend rights, and the judgment
of Mr. John Dillon—and we have his authority for stat-
ing that, according to that judgment, he always cham-
pioned Catholic interests—the judgment of Mr. John
Dillon was such that, though a large shareholder in the
Great Northern Railway, he never entered a protest
against the awful anti-Catholic state of things revealed
on the face of the details of the staffing of that railway ;
and Mr. Dillon, a large shareholder, it must be remem-
bered, had not only the responsibility attached to a
shareholder, but the special responsibility of one who
publicly proclaims that, “according to the best of my
judgment from my earliest years to this day, I have en-
deavoured to champion Catholic interests.’’

Mr. John Dillon is, perhaps, too long in modern Irish
politics to permit one to expect that he should be an
exact thinker or a careful weigher of phrases. Mr. John
Dillon declared : — And to-day in America,
in Canada, in Great Britain itself, and in Australia and
New Zealand, whatever there is of Catholicity is Irish
Catholicity.”” Poor Germany and other countries that
supply Catholics to the world are completely blotted out
by this enthusiastic life-long champion of Catholic in-
terests, this large shareholder in the Great Northern
Railway of Ireland, whose judgment never dictated to
him that he should raise his voice in the boardroom of
that railway company on behalf of his oppressed and
boycotted co-religionists! ;

Mr. John Dillon very properly said :—“ Now, what is
the lesson we have learned, or ought to haye learned,
from persecution? It is this—that you cannot build
up any permanent prosperity or National greatuess on a
basis of intolerance, narrow-mindedness, or persecution.’’
Quite so. But why does not Mr. John Dillon throw in
his lot with those who are fighting for Tolerance, Justice,
and Fair Play? Why did he not, as a large shareholder,
protest against intolerance, narrow-mindedness, an L per-
secution in the staffing of the Great Northern Ra.llw\a.y,

THE TECHNICAL INSTRUCTION CONGRESS.
O have attended a Congress on three consecutive
days, to have voted on twenty or thirty resolu-
tions and amendments, and to have listened to the dis-
cussions upon them, without once attempting a speech
or venturing a remark, was a feat of which I feel justly
proud, and for which, I may add, the Congress ought
to feel deeply grateful. Whilst the example might
without serious consequences have been more widely
followed than it was, yet, on the whole, the talk was
practical and instructive.

It goes without saying that such annual gatherings as
that of the Technical Instruction Congress at Limerick
last weele serve a most useful purpose. To those who
take an active interest in the promotion of Technical
Education, whether as members of Managing Com-
mittees or Principals of Schools, the proceedings at
Limerick this year were of considerable importance.
The time they occupied will have been well spent if the
deliberations and conclusions of the Congress, through
the influence of the delegates, be followed up in the
various districts, and, through the influence of its
special reprosentatives, be taken to heart by the De-
partment. Both the Department and the National
Board sent official representatives to the Congress. It

was a clever move on the part of the Department. It

was a particularly clever move to send Mr. Fletcher.
The innovation, apart from the assistance which Mr.

Fletcher and the other officials were able to give the

whether it is proving, or is likely to prove, of real value

try. The proceedings of the Congress certainly af-

Those proceedings, furthermore, are calculatet'i, I be-

adapted to the circumstances and requirements of the
country.  Experience, above all things, will teach us.

measurable service.

There is, I think, room for improvement in the work-
ing of the Congress itself. At one of its meetings each
year it resolves itself into * The lrish Technical Instruc-
tion Association.”” That organisation’s report is read,
and its officers, Standing Council and Committees are
clected. But there are, scemingly, no regular minutes
of proceedings. There are no standing orders. No re-
ports are printed or placed in the hands of the mem-
bers. It would have been of assistance to have had be-
fore us some vecord of last year’s Congress. One resolu-
tion on the agenda, for instance, proposed to rescind a
former resolution, which was referred to by number
only, and the purport of which was not stated. These,
no doubt, are minor defects, but they are unbusiness-
like, and might easy lead to confusion and unpleasant-
ness. Then, too, something might be done in the way
of getting through the business in a shorter time than
three days. “ All work and no play makes Jack a dull
boy,”” but to limit the sittings of the Congress to three
hours a day scems to me to be letting the delegates off
too lightly. They were treated right royally at Lime-
rick, and had a most enjoyable time, yet I would be for
working them a little harder on future occasions.

The Standing Council, which will meet periodically in
Dublin during the next twelve months, has much use-
ful and practical work before it. With the help and
co-operation of Father Dowling, the genial and inde-
fatigable Hon. Secretary, whose untiring efforts on be-
half of the Congress and the cause of Technical Instruc-
tion throughout Ireland are beyond praise, there is
every reason to hope that no effort will be spared to
reap to the full the fruits of the Limerick Congress.

PATRIOTISM AND PERSONAL PRONOUNS. 
\
~- l HEN the average Briton desires to expound his 
1 V 
views as to his fellow-countrymen and their 
conduct in diverse matters, he invariably tells his lis-
tener that" \Ve, sir, thi.nk so aud so," or" vVe, sir, do 
this and that." He almost invariably identifies him-
self with his compatriots, and his chest expands with 
national pride as he talks of "we" and " us." 
Of 
course if he is speaking in a mere party sense, he will 
drop this form of expression and disassociate himself 
from Conservatives or Liberals according to his personal 
convictions; but when referring to his country as a 
whole, he rarely fails to include himself as one of the 
people to whom his remarks apply. 
This characteris-
tic, while it has its aggressive features, and may n~t 
tend in all cases to enhance the charm of converse, is 
an indication of patriotic feeling, and shows a proper 
pride on the part of the speaker, which deserves, and 
usually, compels respect. It implies an identi:fica~ion 
of the individual· with his fellow-countrymen, and 1s a 
manifestation of the fact that" he is not ashamed to 
rank himself with them. 
It is far from uncommon, on the other hand, for the 
Irishman, talking of !vis fellow countrymen, to r_efer to 
them in the third person plural. It would _be gomg to_o 
far no doubt to say that the average Irishman hesi-
tat~s or refuse~ to speak of the people of .his own nation 
as " we " or " us," but it is not too much to say th~t a 
very large percentage of Iri~hmen eschew th~s: particu-
lar pronouns and by employmg less comprom1smg forms 
of speech convey the impression that the people ~nder 
discussion are another race from themselves. This p~-
culiarity of idiom manifests itself, of C?~rse, rather m 
private conversation than on the poht1c~l platfor:'11. 
From the latter the Irish speaker addressmg an Irish 
audience never fails to express himself in language 
clearly indicating that he and his h~arers al·e of one 
nationality. 
Very likely, too, when _m other an~ less 
public spheres, he is not less unambiguous. It 1s th_e 
man-in-the-street, however, not the man-on-the-plat-
form who is the culprit in this respect. 
~oo often he 
affects au attitude of detachment fro~ his surround-
inas and talks in an impersonal stram. 
'i'fhiuking imperially, as illustriou~ examples show u~, 
leads to talking imperially, and, vice versti, the. ha}l~t 
of talking impersonally begets the faculty of t_hrnkmg 
impersonally. 
Tlrns the Irishm~n who has .~~qmr~,d the . 
knack of referring to other Irishman as 
~hey 
and • 
not as "we," insensibly grows to regard himself as a 
creature apart. 
Hence he is not moved_ to anger. when 
slights are cast upon his country, he 1s not qmck to 
.resent insults offered to men of his own nationality. 
Bei;lg patriotic after a fashion, he may, indeed, argue 
the case on behalf of the maligned, pointing out that 
" they " are not so qad as represented, " they " are not 
so incapable, superstitious, or improvident, as the case 
may be, acco.rding to whatever slander happens to be 
most in vogue at the time. 1'hroughout it all, however, 
he feels no personal sting, the reproaches do not come 
home to him as one of those attacked; he invariably 
replies that "we" are not what our detractors main-
tain. 
Perhaps it may be contended that the habit of mind 
is accountable for the form of speech rather than the 
converse proposition. • It will be urged that it is among 
tlrn,t large class of so-called "Irishmen," aliens to their 
country in all save birth, that such practices alone ob-
(;ain. 
vVere that really so, no need would exist to 
draw attention to the subject. The idiosyncracies of 
that class, daily dwindling in the esllimation of Celt 
and Saxon, are hardly worth troubling about on this 
particular score. 
But is is a strange fact, and one to be 
regretted, that many Irishmen professedly sympathetic 
and even national in their views, have acquired this 
trick of the tongue with the unhappy denationalising 
results I have endeavoured to pourtray. Let us, for 
instance, imagine a discussion between an Irishman of 
this type, and au Englishman on the subject of Home 
Rule. It is no stretch of the imagination to fancy 
that the latter will argue that "we," meaning himself 
and his fellow-countrymen, are b01m rulers and are en-
dowed in a special way by Providence with exceptional 
qualities of wisdom and justice, while the Irishman will 
reply that " they," meaning his countrymen but exclud-
ing himself, might develop these same qualities if given 
a :6,tting opportunity for exercising them. 
Throughout 
the entire argument the Saxon will identify himself 
with his nation; the · Irishman will endeavour to hold 
the scales impartially as a disinterested person. 
These marks do not apply ·to all Irishmen; they may 
not, I hope, be justly applied to the majority of Irish-
men. 
Yet there are enough of us-I thus conform to 
my own rules of grammar- who preserve this attitude 
of aloofness wµen discussing our country and its affairs, 
and, small as ·the matter may appear, it is a mistake 
which we ought to rectify.
